cards.text: 1.2 million CIMB customer records were listed for sale on a cybercrime forum. CIMB denied it with a tweet. One month later, four regulators have said nothing.
cards.sub: On March 2, 2026, a user on Breachforums listed 1.2 million CIMB records — names, account numbers, card data, mobile numbers. CIMB denied the breach on X two days later, stating 'all systems are secure.' As of April 6: no independent forensic audit, no statement from Bank Negara, NACSA, MCMC, or the Personal Data Protection Commissioner.

cards.h: Malaysia's mandatory 72-hour breach notification law has been in effect since June 2025. The CIMB incident is its first real test — and nobody is checking.
cards.s: The PDPA Amendment Act 2024 requires breach notification to the Commissioner within 72 hours and to affected individuals within 7 days if significant harm is likely. The Cyber Security Act 2024 requires NCII entities (including banks) to report incidents to NACSA within 6 hours. If CIMB determined no breach occurred, the question is what investigation methodology was used — and whether any regulator validated it. Bank Negara ordered an independent forensic investigation when iPay88 was breached in 2022. No equivalent action has been taken here.
cards.d: PDPA Amendment 2024; Cyber Security Act 2024; BNM iPay88 directive 2022; Malay Mail Mar 2026

cards.h: Malaysia's average breach detection time is 277 days. Only 2% of organisations are at 'Mature' cybersecurity readiness. A denial is not a diagnosis.
cards.s: IBM's Cost of a Data Breach Report found Malaysian organisations take an average of 277 days to identify and contain a breach — 83 days longer than the global average. Cisco's 2024 Cybersecurity Readiness Index found only 2% of Malaysian organisations at 'Mature' readiness; 66% were at 'Formative' or 'Beginner' stage. Reported data breach cases surged 1,192% from 50 in 2022 to 646 in 2023.
cards.d: IBM 2022; Cisco 2024; Digital Ministry Oct 2024; CyberSecurity Malaysia 2025

cards.h: MySejahtera: denied, then confirmed. Maybank 2022: denied, never verified. CIMB 2026: denied, no audit. The pattern is the policy.
cards.s: In 2023, the Auditor-General confirmed 3 million MySejahtera user records were downloaded by a 'Super Admin' — after the government denied any breach. In December 2022, Maybank denied a breach when 13 million records surfaced online; no independent verification was ever conducted. CIMB's denial follows the same template: assert security, provide no evidence, face no regulatory consequence. Malaysia has passed world-class data protection laws. It has not changed institutional behaviour.
cards.d: Auditor-General Feb 2023; Malay Mail Dec 2022; The Star Mar 2026; DLA Piper 2025

cards.h: The question is not whether the breach is real. It is whether Malaysia's new data protection framework works when the entity denying it is a systemically important bank.
cards.text: Breachforums has a history of both genuine and fabricated listings. The data may or may not be authentic. But that uncertainty is precisely why independent verification matters. A bank with 8 million Malaysian customers cannot be judge and jury of its own data security — not when the law requires 72-hour notification and four regulators have the authority to demand proof.

Malaysia's PDPA amendments and Cyber Security Act 2024 are genuine progress — mandatory notification, enhanced penalties, DPO requirements. But the CIMB incident reveals the enforcement gap: when a Tier-1 bank denies a breach, who verifies the denial? The iPay88 precedent in 2022 showed BNM can order independent audits. The silence since March 2026 shows it chooses not to. Until regulators treat denial-without-evidence as a red flag rather than a resolution, Malaysia's data protection framework will remain impressive on paper and absent in practice.

sources: The Star Mar 2026, The Edge Mar 2026, FMT Mar 2026, Bernama Mar 2026, Lowyat Mar 2026, Business Today Mar 2026, Darknetsearch Mar 2026, IBM 2022/2025, Cisco 2024, Digital Ministry Oct 2024, CyberSecurity Malaysia 2025, BNM iPay88 2022, DLA Piper 2025, Chambers PDPA 2026
confidence: Medium — the breach claim itself is unverified and platform has documented fabricated listings; however the regulatory silence and verification gap are independently verifiable facts
